metadata.summary: Structured 5-category risk identification and scoring using probability x impact matrices with one owner, one trigger, and one concrete mitigation per risk.

content.title: Risk Assessment
content.summary: A structured 5-category (market/product/team/financial/operational) risk identification and scoring process using probability x impact matrices. Each risk gets one owner, one measurable trigger, and a concrete mitigation action (not "monitor + diversify"). Run as a multi-pass agent pipeline: enumerate → red-team pre-mortem → score → human review → convert high-priority risks into SDD tasks.

why.p: Entrepreneurs are either blindly optimistic or paralyzed by fear because there is no structured process for identifying and managing business risks. H/M/L scoring is useful only for relative ranking — absolute probabilities are wrong by an order of magnitude. The pre-mortem template is the highest-leverage artifact: a 30-minute exercise surfaces omissions that a solo risk review misses due to optimism bias.

list.item: Pre-launch go/no-go review for a new product, feature, or market entry
Investor or due-diligence ask requiring a credible risk register plus mitigations
Quarterly business review where assumptions need re-validation
After a near-miss incident — formalizing what almost killed the business
Pivot decision comparing risk profiles of two strategic options
New regulatory exposure appears on the roadmap (GDPR, AI Act, SOC2, payment rails)
Idea-stage with fewer than 3 customer conversations — risk theater is procrastination
Tactical sprint planning — use an issue tracker, not a risk register
One-person side projects with under $1k at stake — overhead exceeds value
When the team will not assign owners or revisit monthly — a static register is worse than none

rule.statement: Enumerate at least 3 risks per category across all 5 categories: market, product, team, financial, operational.
rule.rationale: Agents over-produce market and product risks (well-represented in training data) and under-produce financial, legal, and vendor risks. A minimum quota per category enforces coverage breadth.

list.item: Demand — no one wants it (market too small, poor timing)
Competition — others win (incumbent copies, well-funded rival)
Pricing — can't make money (commoditization, price war)
Trend — market shifts (technology obsolescence)
Technical — can't build it (complexity, dependencies)
Usability — users can't use it (poor UX, learning curve)
Value — doesn't solve problem (feature gaps, wrong solution)
Quality — doesn't work well (bugs, performance issues)
Skills — lack capabilities (missing expertise)
Capacity — not enough bandwidth (founder burnout, solo limits)
Alignment — different goals (co-founder conflict)
Dependency — key person risk (single point of failure)
Runway — running out of money (cash burn too high)
Revenue — can't generate income (monetization fails)
Costs — expenses too high (infrastructure, CAC)
External — economic downturn (recession, funding winter)
Vendor — dependency fails (API discontinued, pricing change)
Legal — compliance issues (GDPR, copyright, licensing)
Security — data breach (hack, data loss)
Scale — can't handle growth (infrastructure breaks)

rule.statement: Score each risk as Probability x Impact where H=3, M=2, L=1. Use scores for relative ranking only — not absolute probability. Cap the active risk register at 10 risks; move everything below score 4 to an Accepted archive section.
rule.rationale: H/M/L scoring is useful for forcing triage, not for computing expected loss. Absolute probabilities require FAIR methodology (quantitative). Capping at 10 prevents the register from becoming a comprehensive but unactionable list.

rule.statement: For each high-priority risk (score 6+), define: one concrete response strategy (Avoid/Mitigate/Transfer/Accept), one specific trigger metric, one named owner, and one measurable action. Reject "monitor + diversify" or "stay agile" as mitigation.
rule.rationale: Generic mitigations are never executed. A trigger metric forces the team to observe a leading indicator; a named owner prevents shared-responsibility diffusion.

list.item: Avoid — eliminate the risk entirely (high prob, high impact)
Mitigate — reduce probability or impact (medium risks)
Transfer — shift to someone else (insurance, contracts)
Accept — live with it (low risks; move to archive)

rule.statement: Review cadence: top 3 risks weekly, full risk register monthly, deep review with external perspective quarterly.
rule.rationale: A 6-month-old risk register reflects a company that no longer exists. Monthly reviews prevent static drift; quarterly outside-view reviews counteract optimism bias that founders apply to their own assessments.

rule.statement: Run a pre-mortem before every major launch: assume the project failed 12 months from today, write a 500-word post-mortem, then convert each failure mode into a risk row.
rule.rationale: Pre-mortems are the highest-leverage risk identification artifact. They surface omissions that a forward-looking risk review misses because they require assuming failure is already a fact, bypassing optimism bias.

section.name: agentic-pipeline

rule.statement: Drive risk assessment as a 4-pass pipeline: (1) researcher agent enumerates candidate risks; (2) red-team agent runs pre-mortem; (3) scorer agent applies prob x impact with citations; (4) human owner reviews and assigns mitigations.
rule.rationale: A single-pass enumeration produces a list anchored on the agent's prior knowledge. A separate red-team agent with a different system prompt (assume failure) surfaces black-swan and tail risks that the enumeration agent's optimism heuristic drops.

rule.statement: Never let an agent autonomously execute an "Avoid" decision. Avoid means killing a feature or market — always require a human-in-the-loop gate before executing.
rule.rationale: Avoiding a risk terminates a product direction. An agent making this call autonomously can invalidate weeks of work. The gate must be explicit and logged.

section.prompt: You are a risk analyst. Given the product brief in <brief>, enumerate risks across 5 categories: market, product, team, financial, operational. For each risk: one-sentence statement, evidence (URL or doc ref), probability (H/M/L), impact (H/M/L). Output as the Risk Register markdown table from risk-assessment/README.md. Minimum 3 risks per category. Cite sources inline.

It is 12 months from today. The launch in <brief> failed. Write a 500-word post-mortem explaining what went wrong. Then convert each failure mode into a risk row (see Pre-Mortem Template). Be specific: no "lack of focus" — name the channel, the hire, the assumption.

rule.statement: Require evidence per row. Reject any risk without a URL, conversation reference, or internal metric.
rule.rationale: Risks without evidence are vibes-based register entries. A URL or document reference forces the agent to ground the risk in observable reality, not inference.

rule.statement: Track mitigation cost vs. expected loss. If a mitigation costs more than prob x impact in dollars, accept the risk instead.
rule.rationale: Teams systematically over-spend on low-EV risks (compliance theater) and under-spend on high-EV risks (channel concentration). The cost comparison provides a decision rule.

rule.statement: Diff the risk register across monthly reviews. The delta — new risks, retired risks, score changes — is the actual signal.
rule.rationale: The absolute register is a point-in-time snapshot. The diff reveals whether identified risks are materializing, being mitigated, or being silently ignored.

rule.statement: Cap at 10 active risks. Anything below score 4 goes to an Accepted archive section, not the live table.
rule.rationale: A register with 30+ items is never reviewed. Focus forces the team to genuinely prioritize.

rule.statement: Owner must be a single named person, not "the team". Each high-priority risk needs one trigger metric per high-priority risk.
rule.rationale: Risks owned by "the team" are unowned. Shared responsibility = no responsibility. A trigger metric makes monitoring automatic, not subjective.

rule.statement: Have someone who is not on the team review the register quarterly. Founders systemically under-rate team and product risks.
rule.rationale: Outside-view reviewer counteracts optimism bias. Internal teams cannot objectively rate their own capabilities or the value of their product.

rule.statement: Tie risks to assumptions. Each risk should reference an explicit assumption from the spec/design — when the assumption is invalidated, the risk changes state automatically.
rule.rationale: Connecting risks to assumptions keeps the register live rather than static. When the assumption is tested and invalidated, the risk row gets a new status without a separate review cycle.

example.title: SaaS Startup Risk Register
example.code:
| Risk | Prob | Impact | Score | Response | |--------------------------|------|--------|-------|-----------| | No product-market fit | H | H | 9 | Mitigate | | Key engineer leaves | M | H | 6 | Mitigate | | AWS costs spike | M | M | 4 | Accept | | Competitor launches | M | M | 4 | Mitigate | | GDPR non-compliance | L | H | 3 | Accept |
Contingency for R1 (No PMF): - Trigger: fewer than 20 paying customers after 6 months - Response: pivot to adjacent problem or different segment - Runway needed: 3 months buffer - Owner: Founder
example.note: Each high-score risk has a concrete trigger metric, not a generic "watch for signals". The trigger is observable, not subjective.

example.title: Content Business Risk Register
example.code:
| Risk | Prob | Impact | Score | Response | |-----------------------------|------|--------|-------|----------| | Platform algorithm change | H | H | 9 | Mitigate | | Creator burnout | M | H | 6 | Mitigate | | Payment processor drops us | L | H | 3 | Accept | | Piracy/content theft | M | L | 2 | Accept |
Contingency for R1 (Platform change): - Trigger: over 30% drop in organic reach over 30 days - Response: shift budget to email, community, paid ads - Resources: 3 months content backlog, ad budget reserved - Owner: Growth Lead
example.note: Trigger is a percentage drop with a time window, not "if reach declines". Measurable and monitorable.

antipattern.description: Too many risks in the register — 30+ active items.
antipattern.reason: Focus on the top 5-10. Everything below score 4 goes to an Accepted archive; it is still tracked but not in the active review table.

antipattern.description: No contingency plans for high-priority risks.
antipattern.reason: Every risk with score 6+ needs a contingency plan with trigger, response, resources, and owner. A risk without a plan is just documentation of a known failure mode.
Founders rate their own product and team risks too low. An outside reviewer quarterly is the minimum check on this bias.

antipattern.description: Probability theater: H/M/L scoring is anchored on gut feel; absolute probabilities are wrong by an order of magnitude.
antipattern.reason: Use it for relative ranking only. Absolute probabilities require FAIR methodology (quantitative loss modeling).

antipattern.description: Black-swan blindness: the framework over-weights known categories (market/product/team/financial/operational). Tail risks (founder health, geopolitics, model regulation) get dropped.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly add a "tail risks" section or use the pre-mortem pass to surface these omissions.

antipattern.description: Mitigation inflation: every risk gets a generic "monitor + diversify" plan that nobody executes.
antipattern.reason: Require an action verb + owner + measurable trigger. Ban "monitor", "diversify", and "stay agile" as sole mitigations.

antipattern.description: Static drift: risk registers age fast — a 6-month-old register reflects a company that no longer exists.
antipattern.reason: Monthly reviews are mandatory. Keep monthly snapshots in git; the delta is the actual signal.

antipattern.description: No ROI on mitigation: cost of mitigation rarely tracked vs. expected loss; teams over-spend on low-EV risks (compliance) and under-spend on high-EV ones (concentration in one channel).
antipattern.reason: If a mitigation costs more than prob x impact in dollars, accept the risk instead.

antipattern.description: LLMs assign H/M/L confidently with no basis. Ungrounded probability scores inflate register quality perception.
antipattern.reason: Force the agent to cite a source or comparable case for any score. Reject rows where the probability is not grounded in evidence or a base rate.

antipattern.description: Category bias: agents over-produce market and product risks and under-produce financial, legal, and vendor risks.
antipattern.reason: Enforce a quota: at least 2 risks per category. Reject submissions missing financial or operational rows.

antipattern.description: Recency bias from web search: researcher agents will pull last week's headlines and over-weight them.
antipattern.reason: Cross-check against historical base rates. A single recent event does not change the underlying probability distribution.

antipattern.description: Optimism leak from system prompts: if the brief is enthusiastic ("groundbreaking platform"), the agent inherits the tone and scores risks lower.
antipattern.reason: Use a neutral, redacted brief for the risk pass, or flip the agent into adversarial role explicitly.

antipattern.description: Mitigation handwaving: agents default to "improve monitoring" / "diversify" / "stay agile" — banned phrases.
antipattern.reason: Require an action verb + owner + measurable trigger. Reject any mitigation that does not name a specific action and a specific person.

antipattern.description: Risk register drift on re-runs: re-running the enumeration agent yields a different ID set every time.
antipattern.reason: Use stable hashes (slug of risk statement) instead of sequential IDs, or have the agent diff-merge instead of regenerate, to preserve register continuity across runs.

antipattern.description: No human checkpoint on "Avoid" decisions. "Avoid" means killing a feature or market.
antipattern.reason: Never let an agent autonomously execute an Avoid response. Hard human-in-the-loop gate required. Log the decision and the reasoning.

antipattern.description: Confidentiality leak: risk statements often contain customer names, vendor terms, or unlaunched plans.
antipattern.reason: Run the scrubber agent before any external sharing or before piping into a 3rd-party SaaS.

antipattern.description: Agent can't run the pre-mortem alone. Pre-mortems need divergent perspectives — solo agent runs collapse to one viewpoint.
antipattern.reason: Use 3+ agents with different system prompts (skeptic, customer, competitor) and consolidate. A single-agent pre-mortem produces a list skewed by the single prompt's optimism level.

script.purpose: Sort risk register by score, lint missing fields and mitigations
